title: Micro-Transaction Merchant Flooding with Card Validation Probing
narrative: Fraudster executes 40-80 transactions at a single merchant within 90-180 minutes, each for $0.50-$3.99, testing stolen card validity before attempting larger fraudulent purchases elsewhere. Pattern emerges at low-risk MCCs (5732 electronics, 5651 family clothing) where small purchases seem plausible. Attacker cycles through 15-25 different email domains while maintaining same card instrument, suggesting automated testing infrastructure. TransactionAmt deliberately stays below fraud alert thresholds ($5) but card1_txn_count_1hour spikes dramatically. Night-time execution (txn_hour 1-5) coincides with reduced merchant fraud monitoring. CVV matches initially but degrades after first 10-15 attempts as fraudster begins guessing. Following this probing phase, validated cards typically appear at different merchants within 6-12 hours for high-value purchases ($400-$1200), creating clear two-stage attack signature.
indicators: card1_txn_count_1hour exceeds 35 transactions at single MID
P_emaildomain changes 15+ times while card1 remains constant
85%+ of transactions occur during txn_hour 1-5 (night window)
CVV match rate drops from 100% to below 40% after transaction 12-15
card1_amt_zscore remains negative (below historic spending pattern)
Same dist1 value across all transactions despite changing email domains
summary: A single card is used for 40-80 tiny purchases ($0.50-$3.99) at one merchant within a few hours, typically overnight, while the email address changes constantly—creating an extreme spike in transaction count with minimal dollar amounts.
reasoning: Criminals are testing whether stolen card numbers work before using them for large fraudulent purchases elsewhere; the micro-amounts avoid triggering fraud alerts while they systematically validate which cards are still active.
indicator_explanations: Card hits the same merchant repeatedly in a concentrated time burst
Transaction amounts stay suspiciously small across all attempts
Email address rotates constantly while the same card keeps trying
Activity clusters in overnight hours when monitoring is weakest
CVV failures spike midway through suggesting guessing or testing phase
Amounts are deliberately kept below cardholder's normal spending threshold
Distance identifier stays fixed despite rotating email addresses
